name: V8 Turboshaft IR
summary: V8's replacement for TurboFan's Sea-of-Nodes IR. Operations live in straight-line basic blocks with dense indexed operand references — closer to LLVM IR than to SoN. 30–40% compile-time reduction on migrated pipelines with parity-or-better code quality on JS workloads.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Turboshaft is V8's replacement for TurboFan's Sea-of-Nodes IR. Where TurboFan stored operations in a graph with implicit ordering (every instruction's position determined by its data dependencies), Turboshaft keeps operations in **straight-line basic blocks** with dense indexed operand references — closer to LLVM IR than to Sea-of-Nodes.

Operations live in a flat buffer with fixed-size slots; operands are 32-bit indices rather than pointers. The data layout is cache-friendly and amenable to per-pass linear iteration.

sections.title: Design trade-offs vs Sea-of-Nodes
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): the V8 team reports a **30–40% compile-time reduction** relative to TurboFan with parity-or-better code quality on the migrated pipelines. Debugging is easier because program order is explicit and cache locality improved by removing pointer-chasing.

Turboshaft's linear shape eliminates global scheduling per pass and gives pass authors predictable iteration behaviour. The cost is less optimisation freedom — instructions cannot freely move across blocks the way Sea-of-Nodes allows. For JIT compilation budgets where compile time dominates, this is the right trade-off; for ahead-of-time optimisation where peak code quality matters more, Sea-of-Nodes still has merit.

sections.title: Industry trend toward linear IRs
sections.content:
The V8 migration from TurboFan to Turboshaft is part of a broader pattern: linear IRs with explicit ordering have become the dominant choice for JIT and many AOT compilers. LLVM's IR is linear; Cranelift's CLIF is linear; PHP 8.4's IR framework is linear. Sea-of-Nodes (HotSpot C2, original TurboFan, GraalVM) remains the outlier — powerful for AOT optimisation but operationally expensive at JIT scale.

The lesson: cache locality and predictable per-pass iteration matter more in practice than the maximum scheduling freedom Sea-of-Nodes offers. For new compiler designs, linear IR is the safer default unless the workload specifically benefits from global scheduling.
